deepseek-r1-distill-llama-8b.text: Okay, so I'm trying to figure out the diagnosis for this patient.
Let me go through the case step by step.
The patient is a 41-year-old man with B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) who underwent an allogeneic stem-cell transplant.
After the transplant, he developed fevers and abdominal pain.
Before the transplant, he had low-grade fevers, fatigue, weight loss, and pain in his right arm and leg.
Imaging showed deep venous thromboses, and his white blood cell count was high with many blasts, confirming the diagnosis of B-cell ALL.
Cytogenetic analysis showed the Philadelphia chromosome, which is typical in B-cell ALL, often associated with a worse prognosis.
He went into remission after induction chemotherapy but then needed a second transplant.
During this period, he was on maintenance chemo with 6-mercaptopurine and dasatinib.
He had a history of IV drug use, which explains the scars and thromboses.
After the second transplant, he developed several complications like mucositis, which is common after high-dose chemo and radiation.
After the transplant, he had some fevers, confusion, and hallucinations.
His weight increased, and he developed facial swelling, mouth ulcers, and edema.
His blood cultures were negative, so infection was less likely.
He had abdominal distension, right lower quadrant pain, guarding, and rebound tenderness, which points towards an abdominal issue.
CT scans showed changes in the appendix area, suggesting appendicitis.
However, surgery wasn't an option due to high risk, so they went for conservative treatment.
The key here is the combination of fever, weight gain, facial swelling, and abdominal signs.
These are classic signs of graft-versus-host disease (GVHD), especially since he's had a stem-cell transplant.
GVHD can involve the skin, gastrointestinal tract, and other organs.
The fact that he's on immunosuppressive therapy post-transplant makes GVHD more likely.
The skin and mouth ulcers, along with systemic symptoms, support this diagnosis.
So, the most likely diagnosis is acute GVHD.
